29:
"And Jerubbaal the son of Joash went and dwelt in his own house." The witnesses note Gideon, the war over, retiring to dwell quietly in his own house. The witnesses note that Gill notes the war being ended, he disbanded his army and retired to his own house at Ophrah. The witnesses note that he is called Jerubbaal, another name of Gideon. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Jerubbaal — gideon; the went and dwelt — in his own house; the refused the crown — sought no power; the content in private — study to be quiet (1 Thess. 4:11).

The witnesses agree Gideon, his work done, sought no power but retired to private life — "went and dwelt in his own house": having refused the crown, he laid down his arms and lived quietly among his people; the contentment of the great deliverer with a private station, after so high a service, marks his true humility and freedom from ambition; it teaches that the servants of God, having done their work, may rest content without grasping for power or honour, "study to be quiet, and to do your own business" (1 Thess. 4:11), Gideon dwelling in his own house.

So the witnesses make the verse dwelt in his own house: The witnesses note the Jerubbaal: Gideon. The witnesses note the went and dwelt: in his own house. 30:
"And Gideon had threescore and ten sons of his body begotten: for he had many wives." The witnesses note Gideon's large household of seventy sons, for he had many wives. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was the number of all his sons; he had children before, as Jether his firstborn. The witnesses note that he had many wives. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the seventy sons — of his body; the a large house — his household; the many wives — the world's custom; the a source of trouble — multiply not wives (Deut. 17:17).

The witnesses agree Gideon's house was large, but the note that "he had many wives" hints at a fault — "threescore and ten sons of his body... for he had many wives": the multiplying of wives, after the manner of the great men of the land, was contrary to God's rule for his people, and his large household would prove a source of trouble; the marring of so good a man's house by this excess shows that even the faithful may follow the customs of the world to their hurt; it teaches that the people of God are not to conform to the world's ways in their families, and that the multiplying of wives breeds sorrow, "neither shall he multiply wives to himself, that his heart turn not away" (Deut. 17:17), Gideon's seventy sons of many wives.

So the witnesses make the verse threescore and ten sons: The witnesses note the seventy sons: of his body. The witnesses note the a large house: his household. The witnesses note the many wives: the world's custom. The witnesses note the a source of trouble: multiply not wives (Deut. 17:17). The expositors observe the seventy sons: of his body. The expositors observe the a large house: his household. The expositors observe the many wives: the world's custom. The expositors observe the a source of trouble: multiply not wives (Deut. 17:17). The verse thus records that Gideon had seventy sons, for he had many wives — the multiplying of wives after the world's manner a fault that would breed trouble, the faithful not to conform to the world, multiplying not wives lest the heart turn away (Deut. 17:17).
31:
"And his concubine that was in Shechem, she also bare him a son, whose name he called Abimelech." The witnesses note Gideon's concubine in Shechem bearing him a son named Abimelech. The witnesses note that Gill notes she was not a harlot, but a secondary wife, of the meaner sort. The witnesses note that the son was named Abimelech. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the his concubine — in Shechem; the bare him a son — abimelech; the my father is king — the ironic name; the the seed of trouble — they shall reap the whirlwind (Hos. 8:7).

The witnesses agree the verse marks the seed of future trouble — "his concubine... bare him a son, whose name he called Abimelech": the son of the Shechemite concubine, whose name means "my father is king," would in time slay his brethren and seize a wicked rule (as the next chapter tells); the bearing of this son out of the irregular union sowed the seed of bitter harvest; it teaches that the disorders of a household bear their fruit in after-trouble, and that sin in the family is visited in the generations, "they have sown the wind, and they shall reap the whirlwind" (Hos. 8:7), Abimelech born of the concubine in Shechem.

So the witnesses make the verse Abimelech born: The witnesses note the his concubine: in Shechem. The witnesses note the bare him a son: Abimelech. The witnesses note the my father is king: the ironic name. The witnesses note the the seed of trouble: they shall reap the whirlwind (Hos. 8:7). The expositors observe the his concubine: in Shechem. The expositors observe the bare him a son: Abimelech. The expositors observe the my father is king: the ironic name. The expositors observe the the seed of trouble: they shall reap the whirlwind (Hos. 8:7). The verse thus records that Gideon's concubine bore him Abimelech — the seed of future trouble sown in an irregular union, sin in the family visited in the generations, sowing the wind and reaping the whirlwind (Hos. 8:7).
32:
"And Gideon the son of Joash died in a good old age, and was buried in the sepulchre of Joash his father, in Ophrah of the Abiezrites." The witnesses note Gideon dying in a good old age and buried with his fathers. The witnesses note that Gill notes he lived some forty years after the war, blessed with a large family, wealth, esteem, and favour with God and men. The witnesses note that he was buried in the sepulchre of his father. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Gideon died — his honoured end; the a good old age — full of years; the buried with his fathers — in Ophrah; the the blessing crowning service — come to thy grave in a full age (Job 5:26).

The witnesses agree Gideon came to an honoured and peaceful end — "died in a good old age, and was buried in the sepulchre of Joash his father": after long years of rest, esteem, and the favour of God and men, the faithful deliverer was gathered to his fathers; the good old age and honourable burial mark the blessing that crowned his service, despite the blot of the ephod; it teaches that God honours his faithful servants with a peaceful end, the righteous coming to the grave in a full age, "thou shalt come to thy grave in a full age, like as a shock of corn cometh in in his season" (Job 5:26), Gideon dying in a good old age.

So the witnesses make the verse a good old age: The witnesses note the Gideon died: his honoured end. The witnesses note the a good old age: full of years. 33:
"And it came to pass, as soon as Gideon was dead, that the children of Israel turned again, and went a whoring after Baalim, and made Baalberith their god." The witnesses note Israel turning again to Baal-worship as soon as Gideon was dead. The witnesses note that Gill notes they turned from God and the pure worship of him to idolatry. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set them making Baal-berith their god. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the as soon as Gideon was dead — the relapse; the went a whoring after Baalim — idolatry; the made Baal-berith their god — the lord of the covenant; the shallow reformation — another generation knew not the LORD (Judg. 2:10).

The witnesses agree the relapse came at once on the deliverer's death — "as soon as Gideon was dead... they... went a whoring after Baalim, and made Baalberith their god": no sooner was the restraint of his presence gone than the people fell back to idolatry, choosing "Baal-berith" (the lord of the covenant) for their god; the swiftness of the turning shows how shallow their reformation had been, held only by their leader; it teaches that the goodness of God's people must be rooted in the heart and not in the presence of a man, lest it fail when the prop is gone, "they served the LORD all the days of Joshua... and when... that generation were gathered... there arose another generation which knew not the LORD" (cf. Judg. 2:10), Israel turning to Baal as soon as Gideon died.

So the witnesses make the verse turned again to Baal: The witnesses note the as soon as Gideon was dead: the relapse. The witnesses note the went a whoring after Baalim: idolatry. 34:
"And the children of Israel remembered not the LORD their God, who had delivered them out of the hands of all their enemies on every side:" The witnesses note Israel forgetting the LORD their God who had delivered them from all their enemies. The witnesses note that Gill notes they forgot and forsook him, which showed base ingratitude. The witnesses note that he had delivered them from all their enemies on every side. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the remembered not the LORD — their God; the who had delivered them — from all enemies; the base ingratitude — forgetting his mercies; the forget not his benefits — bless the LORD, forget not his benefits (Ps. 103:2).

The witnesses agree the root of the relapse was forgetfulness of God — "remembered not the LORD their God, who had delivered them": the people forgot the God who had saved them from every foe, base ingratitude marking their fall; the forgetting of so great a Deliverer shows how the heart, once turned from God, loses the very memory of his mercies; it teaches that to forget the LORD and his benefits is the beginning of apostasy, and that his people must keep his mercies ever in mind, "bless the LORD, O my soul, and forget not all his benefits" (Ps. 103:2), Israel remembering not the LORD their deliverer.

So the witnesses make the verse remembered not the LORD: The witnesses note the remembered not the LORD: their God. The witnesses note the who had delivered them: from all enemies. 35:
"Neither shewed they kindness to the house of Jerubbaal, namely, Gideon, according to all the goodness which he had shewed unto Israel." The witnesses note Israel showing no kindness to Gideon's house, despite all his goodness to them. The witnesses note that Gill notes they showed great unkindness and cruelty, slaying his seventy sons, as related in the next chapter. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set their failure to show kindness to his house. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the shewed no kindness — to Gideon's house; the according to all his goodness — their ingratitude; the forgetting God, forgetting his servant — the hardened heart; the unthankfulness breeds cruelty — unthankful, without natural affection (2 Tim. 3:2-3).

The witnesses agree the people's ingratitude reached even to their deliverer's family — "neither shewed they kindness to the house of... Gideon, according to all the goodness which he had shewed": forgetting God, they forgot also his servant, and would soon suffer the slaughter of Gideon's sons under Abimelech (as the next chapter tells); the ingratitude to the family of their benefactor shows how forsaking God hardens the heart against all goodness; it teaches that those who are unthankful to God become unthankful to men, and that the forgetting of mercy breeds cruelty, "men shall be... unthankful, unholy, without natural affection" (2 Tim. 3:2-3); thus the chapter closes, Israel showing no kindness to the house of Gideon.

The witnesses note the forgetting God, forgetting his servant: the hardened heart. The witnesses note the unthankfulness breeds cruelty: unthankful, without natural affection (2 Tim. 3:2-3).
